title: Ground-state energies of Ising models calculated using the samples from a quantum computer that simulates short-time evolution
authors: John P. T. Stenger, C. Stephen Hellberg, Daniel Gunlycke
abstract: We find the ground-state energy of the Ising model using the Cascaded Variational Quantum Eigensolver (CVQE) algorithm with the Guided-Sampling Ansatz (GSA) using up to 63 qubits on a quantum computer. We study a heavy-hex lattice to match the qubit architecture, allowing us to perform calculations in the quantum utility regime. We study both a homogeneous and random-coupling model. We locate the boundary of acceptable quantum errors as a function of the number of qubits and coupling strength. An entropic analysis is performed giving insights into the quantum computing performance. A subspace analysis is performed that suggests that the Ising model is especially suited for near-term quantum computing.
summary: This paper presents a method to calculate the ground-state energies of Ising models using a quantum computer to simulate short-time evolution. By using the CVQE algorithm on up to 63 qubits, the authors demonstrate that the required computational subspace grows slowly with system size. They identify how hardware errors scale with coupling strength and qubit count, and propose an information ratio as a diagnostic for calculation accuracy. The work highlights the potential for near-term quantum devices to perform useful simulations of spin systems.
main_problem: Estimating ground-state energies of Ising models using short-time evolution on near-term quantum hardware while managing the impact of hardware errors and decoherence.
main_result: The CVQE algorithm effectively finds ground-state energies for up to 63 qubits, demonstrating that the number of significant basis states scales subexponentially, though errors increase with qubit number and coupling strength.
method: The Cascaded Variational Quantum Eigensolver (CVQE) uses a Guided-Sampling Ansatz (GSA) where short-time adiabatic state preparation generates a subspace for classical eigenvalue solving.
model_or_system: Transverse-field Ising models (both homogeneous and random-coupling/spin-glass) on a heavy-hex lattice, implemented on the IBM ibm_torino quantum processor.
key_observables: Ground-state energy (E0), average spin (sZ), information ratio (R), and number of significant basis states (N_psi).
important_parameters: Coupling strength (J0 or delta J), transverse field (B), number of qubits (NQ), and the ratio of coupling to field strength.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that short-time diabatic evolution can capture the ground state within a manageable subspace and that the heavy-hex architecture can be used to reach the quantum utility regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows lattice connectivity; Figure 2 plots average spin, energy, and information ratio for homogeneous models; Figure 3 shows similar metrics for random-coupling models; Figure 4 compares basis state probability distributions between exact and CVQE results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CVQE algorithm and GSA, applies it to homogeneous and random Ising models on IBM hardware, performs error and entropic analysis, provides a mathematical framework for diabatic evolution, and concludes with scaling and utility assessments.
why_interesting: It provides a practical framework for using short-coherence-time devices to study many-body dynamics and ground states, specifically showing how to use diabatic processes to bypass the need for full adiabaticity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Heavy-hex lattice. The circles represent lattice sites and the lines represent connectivity. We describe the size of a lattice by the number of layers in the x-direction Nx and the number of layers in the y-direction Ny. An example Nx = 7, Ny = 7 lattice is outlined by the dashed lines.

Figure 2. Results for the homogeneous coupling model. (a) the average spin as a function of the coupling strength J0 for various lattice sizes. The total number of qubits NQ = 18, 23, 34, 40, 55, 63 corresponds to lattice sizes of Nx = Ny = 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 respectively. (b) Effective ground-state energy E1 0 as a function of the total number of qubits NQ and the coupling strength J0. (c) the information ratio R as a function of the total number of qubits NQ and the coupling strength J0.

Figure 3. Results from the random-coupling model. (a) the average spin as a function of the coupling-strength bound ∆J for various lattice sizes. The total number of qubits NQ = 18, 23, 34, 40, 55, 63 corresponds to lattice sizes of Nx = Ny = 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 respectively. (b) Effective ground-state energy E1 0 as a function of the total number of qubits NQ and the coupling-strength bound ∆J. Figure 4 shows the basis state structure for the ground state under several different scenarios. The panels on the left hand side (Fig. 4a,b,c) show data from an ex- actly solved square lattice model where we average over the results of 1000 different random couplings. Fig. 4a shows plots of the number of basis states with a proba- bility above P Ψ s > 0.1/|B1| as a function of the number of spins NQ. For ∆J/B < 1 we see that the number of significant basis states grows nearly linearly with the size of the system. As we increase ∆J/B > 1 we see that the number of significant basis states also increases. How- ever, the dependence on NQ does not surpass cubic even for the largest coupling...

Figure 4. Basis state structure of the ground state. (a),(b),(c) are for a small square lattice model exactly solved and averaged over 1000 different random couplings. (d),(e),(f) are for the homogeneous model. (g),(h),(i) are for the random-coupling model. (a),(d),(g) show the number of basis states Nψ that have a probability in the ground state greater than P Ψ s > 0.1/|B1| as a function of NQ for select values of the coupling J0 or ∆J. The dashed, dotted, and dash-dot lines represent linear NQ, square N 2 Q, and cubic N 3 Q boundaries respectively. (b),(e),(h) show the number of basis states Nψ that have a probability in the ground state greater than P Ψ s > 0.1/|B1| as a function of the...

Fig. 4b also shows the total number of basis states with P Ψ s > 0.1/|B1| but as a function of the coupling ∆J and divided by NQ. We see that the number of basis states with probability above the cutoff increases incrementally. This is due to the small system size, as the probability distribution is discrete. This is seen clearly in Fig. 4c where we plot the probability of each basis state sorted into bins of total spin. Notice that there is a single basis state with total spin equal to 9 with the highest prob- ability.
